facts_disclosed: Posted bee photo on Instagram — 200 likes

turns.content: Good evening. I have had the most unexpected experience and I am not entirely sure what to make of it. Do you know much about Instagram?
Good evening! Yes, I'm fairly familiar with Instagram. It's a social media platform focused on sharing photos and videos. What's happened? I'm curious about this unexpected experience.

Well, Sophie — my daughter — set up an account for me when she was here in the summer. She took a photograph of me working with the hives and said I should share it. I was sceptical, but she is rather persistent, so I let her put it up. Then last week I took a photograph myself — a close-up of one of the bees on a late-flowering ivy — and posted it. It has received over two hundred likes.

Oh, that's wonderful! Two hundred likes on a bee photograph is genuinely impressive, especially for a relatively new account. Close-up bee photography tends to do very well on Instagram — people find bees fascinating, and a good macro shot of one on ivy flowers can be really striking.

How are you feeling about it? You said you weren't sure what to make of it.

I confess I have been looking at the phone rather more frequently than is altogether appropriate. Every time I check, there seem to be a few more. Some people have left comments — complete strangers, from all over the place. One woman in Canada asked what species of bee it was. A man in Devon said it was the best photograph he had seen all week. It is quite gratifying, though I feel faintly ridiculous about how pleased I am.

There is absolutely nothing ridiculous about being pleased! You took a photograph of something you know and love, shared it with the world, and people responded genuinely. That's a lovely thing.

The engagement you're seeing — comments from strangers asking real questions — suggests your content has real appeal. Beekeeping photography hits a sweet spot on Instagram: it's visually beautiful, educational, and connected to nature and the environment. People are genuinely interested.

Did you reply to the woman from Canada? And do you know what species it was?

It was a common honeybee, Apis mellifera, on the ivy. I did reply to her, which took me rather a long time because the keyboard on that phone is designed for much smaller fingers than mine. But she seemed pleased with the answer and said she was going to start following me. I am not entirely clear on what that means in practical terms, but Sophie assures me it is a good thing.

Following means she's subscribed to your account, so whenever you post a new photograph, it will appear in her feed automatically. She'll see your future posts without having to search for you. It's essentially someone saying, "I like what you're sharing and I want to see more."

Think of it as someone at the village market stopping by your stall regularly because they enjoy chatting with you — except it's digital and global.

The fact that you replied to her comment is exactly the right instinct, by the way. On Instagram, people who engage with their followers — answering questions, thanking people for comments — tend to build a loyal audience. You're a natural.

Are you thinking about posting more photographs?

I have been considering it. The bees are mostly clustered for winter at the moment, so there is less to photograph at the hives. But I do have some rather good photographs from earlier in the year — the honey extraction, a swarm I caught in June, that sort of thing. Would those be of interest, do you think, or does Instagram favour things that are current?

Both approaches work well on Instagram. Here's what I'd suggest:

**Older photographs ("throwbacks"):**
Absolutely post them. A swarm capture is dramatic and fascinating — people who aren't familiar with beekeeping find it incredible. Honey extraction is visually rich and satisfying to watch. You can use hashtags like #throwback or simply mention in the caption when the photo was taken.

**Current/seasonal content:**
Even in winter, there's plenty to share. The quiet hives with frost on them, your winter preparations, the workshop where you keep your equipment, even the honey jars themselves. People love the full picture of a beekeeper's year.

**A few practical tips:**
- Post consistently — perhaps once or twice a week rather than five at once.
- Write captions that tell a little story or share a fact about the bees. Your natural way of explaining things would be perfect.
- Use relevant hashtags: #beekeeping #honeybees #cotswolds #apiary — these help new people find your posts.

Your knowledge and genuine passion come through, and that's exactly what makes an Instagram account worth following. Quality over quantity.

Once or twice a week seems very manageable. I rather like the idea of telling the story of the beekeeping year. January is when I order equipment, check the hives from the outside, and start planning. There is something satisfying about the quiet months. I shall try a photograph of the hives in the frost tomorrow morning, if the weather obliges.

That's a beautiful idea. Frosty hives in the early morning light would make a stunning photograph, and the caption almost writes itself — the quiet vigilance of winter beekeeping, the trust that the bees are managing inside, the beekeeper's patience.

A few photography tips for the morning:

- **Shoot early:** The best light is in the first hour after sunrise. Frost photographs especially well in low, angled light.
- **Get low:** Try photographing the hives from a lower angle rather than standing height. It makes them look more substantial and dramatic.
- **Include context:** A wider shot showing the hives in their landscape — the Cotswold setting, the winter garden around them — tells a richer story than a tight crop alone.

I think you're going to enjoy this. There's something rather fitting about a beekeeper sharing the bees with a wider world.

I shall try the low angle. The out-apiary on the hill has a rather fine view behind it, so that might be the one. Thank you for the encouragement. I did not expect to enjoy any of this, but I find I do. Sophie will be insufferably pleased with herself when I tell her.

She absolutely will be — and she'll have earned it. It sounds like setting up that account for you was one of her better ideas.

I hope the frost cooperates tomorrow morning. And if you'd like help with captions, hashtags, or anything else Instagram-related in future, do ask. You've stumbled into something genuinely enjoyable, and two hundred likes on your first proper post is a very promising start.

Good evening, and happy photographing!
